_note: Hand-drafted spec for the LRU Cache simulation. Uses simulation.type='data-structure' to show the schema generalizes beyond layered-stack (RPC) simulations. Use with /sim-from-spec or specs/SIMULATION-SKILL.md to generate src/topics/lru-cache/LruCacheDemo.jsx.

meta.title: LRU Cache
meta.description: Interact with a live LRU cache — watch evictions happen as the cache fills, and see hit rates improve as access patterns warm up.
concepts: An LRU (Least Recently Used) cache evicts the item that was accessed least recently when capacity is exceeded
The doubly-linked list tracks recency: head is most-recently-used (MRU), tail is least-recently-used (LRU)
A hash map provides O(1) lookup of any node's position in the linked list
A cache hit moves the node to the head; a cache miss inserts a new node at the head (and may evict the tail)
Hit rate = hits / (hits + misses); warming up access patterns increases hit rate

trigger.description: User clicks a key button (or types a key) to simulate cache.get(key) or cache.put(key, value)

get.label: get(key)
get.note: If key is in cache (HIT): move to MRU head, return value. If not (MISS): insert at head, evict LRU tail if over capacity.

reset.label: Reset
reset.note: Clear the cache and reset hit/miss counters

states.idle: Default node — muted border, normal background
states.hit: Cache hit — green accent, briefly pulses
states.miss: Cache miss / new insert — cyan accent, briefly pulses
states.evicted: Node being evicted — red accent, fades out
states.moved: Node being repositioned to head — yellow accent, briefly pulses

onCacheHit.note: Highlight key in hash map — show pointer to node
Mark node as 'hit' (green) in linked list
Unlink node from current position (update prev.next and next.prev)
Relink node at head (after HEAD sentinel)
Increment hit counter; show updated list order

onCacheMiss.note: Highlight hash map — key not found
Insert node at head (after HEAD sentinel)
Update hash map entry — key → new node
If size > capacity: mark tail node as 'evicted' (red), remove from list and hash map
Increment miss counter; show updated list order

Linked List.title: Doubly-Linked List
Linked List.body: The **doubly-linked list** tracks access order. The **head** holds the most-recently-used (MRU) item; the **tail** holds the least-recently-used (LRU) item.
Linked List.more: When a key is accessed (hit or miss), its node moves to the head. When the cache is full, the tail node is evicted. Doubly-linking lets every insert, remove, and move happen in **O(1)** time because you can update prev/next pointers directly without scanning the list. The HEAD and TAIL are **sentinel nodes** — they never hold real data and make edge-case pointer manipulation simpler.

Hash Map.title: Hash Map
Hash Map.body: The **hash map** maps each key to its **node** in the linked list, enabling **O(1) lookup** without scanning the list.
Hash Map.more: Without the hash map, finding a node in the linked list would cost O(n). With it, every `get` and `put` operation is O(1): look up the key in the hash map to get a direct pointer to the node, then perform the O(1) list operation. This combination — hash map + doubly-linked list — is the classic LRU cache implementation used in production systems and is also the basis for Java's `LinkedHashMap`.

Stats.title: Cache Stats
Stats.body: **Hit rate** = hits ÷ (hits + misses). Each **cache hit** increments the hit counter; each **miss** increments the miss counter.
Stats.more: In real systems, hit rate is the primary cache health metric. A **cold cache** starts at 0% hit rate and warms up as popular keys are accessed repeatedly. The hit rate depends on the **access pattern** (how skewed requests are toward a small working set) and the **cache capacity** relative to the working set size. This simulation lets you observe how different access patterns affect hit rate in real time.

ui.emptyState: Cache is empty — click a key to start

guide.title: LRU Cache

lines: An **LRU (Least Recently Used) cache** is a fixed-size key-value store that automatically evicts the item accessed **longest ago** when new items need space.

In **system design**, LRU caches appear everywhere — from CPU L1/L2 caches to CDN edge caches to application-layer caches like Redis. They work well when **access patterns are temporal** (recently accessed data is likely to be accessed again).
The classic implementation combines a **doubly-linked list** (for O(1) eviction) with a **hash map** (for O(1) lookup), giving every operation constant time.
introduction.simulationLine: Click the key buttons to simulate **cache.get(key)** operations. Watch hits, misses, and evictions happen in real time.

paragraphs: The LRU eviction policy is popular because it approximates the **optimal offline eviction policy** (Belady's algorithm) when access patterns are temporal. It's not perfect — it struggles with **one-time sequential scans** that thrash the cache — but it's simple, predictable, and works well for most real-world workloads.

The doubly-linked list maintains strict access order by physically moving the accessed node to the head on every hit. This is more expensive than just updating a timestamp (O(1) pointer ops vs O(1) integer write), but it keeps eviction O(1) — you always evict the tail, no scanning needed.

Production caches like **Redis** and **Memcached** use variations of LRU. Redis actually uses a **probabilistic LRU** — instead of maintaining a perfect doubly-linked list across all keys (expensive at scale), it samples a small pool of random keys and evicts the least-recently-used among the sample. This trades a small accuracy loss for dramatically lower memory and CPU overhead.
